examples.title: Advisory File Locking with flock()
examples.description: Example showing how to use flock() for advisory file locking in UNIX
examples.code:
BEGIN
 fd = open("shared_file.txt", O_RDWR)
 IF fd == -1 THEN
 PRINT "Failed to open file"
 EXIT
 ENDIF

 // Acquire exclusive lock (blocking)
 IF flock(fd, LOCK_EX) == -1 THEN
 PRINT "Failed to acquire lock"
 EXIT
 ENDIF

 // Perform file operations
 data = read(fd, buffer, SIZE)
 modified_data = process(data)
 write(fd, modified_data, SIZE)

 // Release lock
 flock(fd, LOCK_UN)
 close(fd)

 PRINT "File operations completed"
END
examples.explanation: flock() provides advisory locking in UNIX. LOCK_EX is exclusive (write) lock, LOCK_UN releases it. Other processes can ignore the lock (advisory), so they must voluntarily check and respect the lock to prevent race conditions.

examples.title: Non-blocking Lock with fcntl()
examples.description: Demonstrating non-blocking lock acquisition using fcntl()
examples.code:
BEGIN
 fd = open("data.txt", O_RDWR)
 IF fd == -1 THEN
 EXIT
 ENDIF

 // Structure for lock request
 lockStruct.l_type = F_WRLCK // Write (exclusive) lock
 lockStruct.l_whence = SEEK_SET
 lockStruct.l_start = 0 // Start of file
 lockStruct.l_len = 0 // 0 means entire file
 lockStruct.l_pid = getpid()

 // Try non-blocking lock
 result = fcntl(fd, F_SETLK, lockStruct)

 IF result == -1 THEN
 // Check errno for reason
 IF errno == EACCES OR errno == EAGAIN THEN
 PRINT "Lock unavailable, another process holds it"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Lock error occurred"
 ENDIF
 close(fd)
 EXIT
 ENDIF

 // Lock acquired - perform operations
 perform_operations(fd)

 // Release lock
 lockStruct.l_type = F_UNLCK
 fcntl(fd, F_SETLK, lockStruct)
 close(fd)
END
examples.explanation: fcntl() with F_SETLK attempts non-blocking lock. Returns immediately with error if unavailable. F_SETLKW would block until lock becomes available. This allows graceful handling when lock cannot be obtained immediately.

examples.title: Readers-Writers Problem (Writer Priority)
examples.description: Semaphore-based solution with writer priority to prevent starvation
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // Shared variables
 readcount = 0
 mutex = Semaphore(1) // Protects readcount
 rw_mutex = Semaphore(1) // For writers and readers-writers sync
 queue = Semaphore(1) // Ensures fairness

 // Reader Process
 PROCEDURE reader()
 wait(queue) // Fair ordering
 wait(mutex) // Protect readcount
 readcount = readcount + 1
 IF readcount == 1 THEN
 wait(rw_mutex) // First reader locks resource
 ENDIF
 signal(queue) // Allow others to queue
 signal(mutex)

 // CRITICAL SECTION: Read file
 read_file()

 wait(mutex)
 readcount = readcount - 1
 IF readcount == 0 THEN
 signal(rw_mutex) // Last reader releases
 ENDIF
 signal(mutex)
 ENDPROCEDURE

 // Writer Process
 PROCEDURE writer()
 wait(queue) // Wait in queue
 wait(rw_mutex) // Get exclusive access
 signal(queue)

 // CRITICAL SECTION: Write to file
 write_file()

 signal(rw_mutex) // Release exclusive access
 ENDPROCEDURE
END
examples.explanation: Writer priority solution: queue ensures fair ordering. When writer waits, new readers must wait in queue after writer, preventing writer starvation. readcount tracks active readers. First reader locks resource, last reader releases. Writers get exclusive access to ensure consistency.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for lock operations, O(n) where n = number of concurrent processes
